institutions.institution_blurb: Member, American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant who married his daughter Dorothy

narrative_blocks.header: His father gave him one week to decide
narrative_blocks.body: His father Ephraim — a stern Puritan militia captain — told him at sixteen that he had one week to decide whether to receive a college education. John accepted before the week expired. He prepared under the Rev. Timothy Pitkin, then entered Yale in 1763.

narrative_blocks.header: Assumed the governorship when the sitting governor died
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Lieutenant Governor for eleven years under Gov. Jonathan Trumbull Jr. On August 7, 1809, Trumbull died in office, and Treadwell assumed the governorship. He was elected in his own right in 1810, receiving 10,265 votes to 7,185 for the Republican candidate.

He negotiated the $1,200,000 sale of northeastern Ohio land to endow the Connecticut School Fund — the permanent foundation for public education in the state. He was appointed to the Board of Managers to administer the Fund and is remembered as "the father of common school education." He also co-founded the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences (incorporated 1799).

He is considered the last Puritan governor of Connecticut — in him, wrote his obituary, "we see blended for the last time the theologian and statesman." He was a founding member and President (1798-1822) of the Connecticut Missionary Society, the oldest missionary society in the United States, sending Congregational ministers to new settlements in Vermont, New Hampshire, and western New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Failed as a merchant — recovered by manufacturing nitre
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale he rejected a law career to work the family farm, then ventured into mercantile trade and failed badly. He recovered by manufacturing nitre (saltpetre), extricating himself from the financial loss. He went on to serve as Farmington's Probate Judge for 26 years, which he treasured for the chance to help widows, orphans, and the less fortunate through wills and estates.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Hooker men married into the Treadwell family
narrative_blocks.body: EH called Mary Treadwell the "daughter" of Gov. John Treadwell — but Treadwell was born in 1745 and she in 1755; he was ten at her birth. Ephraim Treadwell's 1781 will confirms both as siblings.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait by George F. Wright, Museum of Connecticut History.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait by George F. Wright, Museum of Connecticut History.

burial.plot_notes: Buried next to husband John Treadwell.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife died the day their son
narrative_blocks.body: Belinda Porter died on February 22, 1792 — the same day their son Ichabod Porter Norton was born. Ichabod survived his mother by just 21 years, dying in 1813.

Two years after Belinda's death, he married Dorothy Treadwell — daughter of Gov. John Treadwell, the 21st Governor of Connecticut. Through that marriage, and through his first cousin once removed Maj. Roger Hooker's marriage to the Governor's sister Mary, two members of the Hooker family had independently married into the Treadwell household.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins once removed — both married into the Treadwell family.
his father-in-law, a governor of Connecticut

narrative_blocks.body: He served in Capt. Lacey's Company (Fourth Regiment, Col. Andrew Ward Jr.) and appears in the Oct 13, 1756 muster roll as "sick at Fort Edward" during the Crown Point campaign. The following year he rode with Capt. Smedley's relief company for 16 days following the French and Indian massacre at Fort William Henry in August 1757.

He is described as "a mechanic of competent fortune" whose wife Mary "were very pious." He served as Ensign in the Farmington militia (October 1750) and rose to Captain by October 1752. He gave his son John one week to decide about college — an offer John accepted before the week expired. John later became the 21st Governor of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1781 will was witnessed by Col. Noadiah
narrative_blocks.body: His will, dated December 18, 1781, was witnessed by three prominent Farmington neighbors: Col. Noadiah Hooker, Martin Bull (, the goldsmith and engraver), and Rebekah Hooker. He appointed his wife Mary as executor and his son John as co-executor — the same John who would become Governor. He died on January 11, 1782, just weeks after signing.

military_service.unit: Captain Lacey's Company, Fourth Regiment (Col. Andrew Ward Jr.); later Capt. Smedley's Company

battles.notes: Fort William Henry, Oct 13, 1756 — sick at Fort Edward
Relief of Fort William Henry, Aug 1757 — 16 days service with Capt. Smedley's Company

narrative_blocks.header: Named executor of her husband's 1781 will
narrative_blocks.body: She and Ephraim "were very pious." When Ephraim signed his will in December 1781, he appointed her as primary executor alongside their son John — who would become Governor of Connecticut seventeen years later. She outlived her husband by sixteen years, dying December 26, 1798 at approximately age 85.

burialCemetery.name: Memento Mori Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Rev. Samuel Hooker buried here; no stone survives.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST072.name: Connecticut Missionary Society
INST072.notes: Founded 1798 — the oldest missionary society in the United States. The missionary arm of the Connecticut General Association of Congregational ministers. Gov. John Treadwell (X02115) was a founding member and served as President 1798-1822. Sent Congregational missionaries to settlements in Vermont, New Hampshire, and western New York to bring New England values and "promote Christian Knowledge."

INST041.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.notes: Founded 1810 at Andover Seminary, Massachusetts by prominent Congregationalists. First American foreign missionary society; first missions to China and Ceylon. Yale President Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) was among the founding figures; Dwight Mission (established 1820 in present-day Arkansas/Oklahoma for the Cherokee) was named in his honor. The board operated Brainerd Mission (Chattanooga TN, est.1817) where Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) and Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) served the Cherokee. HOOKER TREE CONNECTIONS: 1. Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) — Yale President 1795-1817; founding ABCFM figure; Dwight Mission named for him. 5th-generation Hooker descendant. 2. Elias Cornelius (X00724) — ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 missionary tour to Creek and Cherokee; m. Mary Ann Hooker (H01355). 3. Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) — corporate ABCFM member from 1840. 4. Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) — served at Brainerd Mission 1821-1837; granddaughter of Rev. Cyprian Strong (H00299). 5. Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) — Brainerd missionary 1822-1837; accompanied Cherokee on Trail of Tears 1838; first mayor of Dalton GA. 6. Dr. Mary Floyd Cushman (HD2818, gen 8) — ABCFM medical missionary in Angola 1922-1953. 7. Samuel Gelston Dwight (HD2376) — ABCFM missionary to Hawaii; children named for ABCFM colleagues Juliette Cooke and Samuel Castle.
